title: Assertions — pick the right check
blurb: The assert is the heart of the test: it's the line that decides pass or fail. Match each xUnit assertion to what it actually verifies.

levels.title: L1 · The everyday asserts
levels.prompt: Connect each xUnit assertion to what it checks.

left.label: Assert.Equal(expected, actual)
left.note: the one you'll reach for most

failStates.title: Assert.True isn't Assert.Equal
failStates.explain: Assert.True only checks a bool. To compare two values use Assert.Equal(expected, actual) — on failure it reports 'Expected 5, Actual 3', where Assert.True(x == y) would only say 'expected True, got False'.

failStates.title: Throws is for exceptions
failStates.explain: Assert.Throws<T> runs the code and passes only if it throws T. It's how you test error paths — not a boolean check.

levels.title: L2 · The ones people mix up
levels.prompt: Same idea, trickier pairs. Match each assertion to what it verifies.

right.label: a and b are the SAME object
a and b have equal values

failStates.title: Same ≠ Equal
failStates.explain: Assert.Same checks reference identity — is it literally the same object in memory? Assert.Equal checks value equality. Two different lists with identical contents are Equal but not Same.

failStates.title: Equal ≠ Same
failStates.explain: Assert.Equal compares values (via Equals). For 'is it the exact same instance?', that's Assert.Same.
